text_clean: Don’t bother. The site is closed and you can’t even walk along the base of the wall. You pretty much can get to a fence near where the entrance would have been. It sounds like it will be literally years at best before the site reopens. None of the guides or tour operators or people running the site want to admit that their ‘premiere site’ for northern Peru is closed to tourism for possibly a decade. I would vaguely guess they will need to build a roof over the entire structure at some point to keep stormwater from destabilizing the wall any further. Unless the want to tear sections of the wall down, put a drainage system behind the wall, and rebuild it with a modern support / drainage system behind the walls. Anyways this section of the country is fantastic, and you should totally visit it. But don’t pretend you are going to be able to walk around the top of Kuelap anytime this decade.

text_clean: ¡Una visita totalmente recomendable!Fuimos un grupo de 4 personas y nos resultó mucho más cómodo y económico desplazarnos desde Trujillo en Uber. El proceso es sencillo: compras la entrada en la boletería, necesitas identificarte (DNI o Pasaporte) y luego caminas unos 200 metros hacia el ingreso. Esa caminata corta es a pleno sol, por lo que es indispensable llevar agua y sombrero, aunque afortunadamente en la entrada misma sí hay sombra para esperar. Esperamos unos 20 minutos a que se formara un grupo para entrar con un guía ( el pago es mediante aportación voluntaria). Un gran detalle es que los mayores de edad cuentan con ticket reducido. Itinerario con rampas, escalones i suelo de arena, no adaptado a sillas de rueddas. La visita dura aproximadamente 1 hora y el recorrido por este templo Moche es súper interesante. Cabe mencionar que la Huaca del Sol no es accesible, pero la Huaca de la Luna compensa todo. ¡Vale muchísimo la pena!
label: positivo

text_clean: Fascinating archaeological site just outside Lima! The museum is modern, clean and very well curated, offering a clear explanation of the history of Pachacamac and the different cultures that lived here — from the Lima and Wari to the Inca civilization. The ruins are vast and impressive, with temples, plazas and stunning views of the valley and the Pacific Ocean. Entry costs S/15 per person, and you can explore the site either by walking or by car. Highly recommended to go with a guide to understand the cultural and spiritual importance of the place.

destination: Huacas del Sol y de la Luna
text_clean: Cuál es la mejor temporada para visitar la Huaca del Sol y la Luna? Se puede visitar durante todo el año. Pero, se tiene que tener en cuenta que durante los meses de enero a marzo el clima en Trujillo es bastante caluroso y durante los meses de abril a diciembre es más fresco.Está ubicada al norte del Perú en el departamento de la libertad, a unos 5 km al sur de Trujillo. Se puede visitar este complejo arqueológico de lunes a domingo de 9:00 a.m. hasta las 4:00 p.m. Los tickets de entrada tienen un precio de S/. 10.00 para adultos. Qué llevar: Calzado cómodo, gorro para el sol, anteojos de sol, bloqueador solar, snack, agua y ropa cómoda y un poco de plata..😋 Es obligatorio tener un documento de vacunación con esquema completo.. En mí caso que vivo en tumbes puedo comprar paquetes online por el Tripavisor ó de lo contrario tomar un bus. Tumbes a Trujillo en Bus Oltursa es la principal empresa de transporte que cuenta con esta ruta en su itinerario, ofreciendo pasajes a precios económicos de 70 y 90 soles. Los buses salen desde el terminal., que queda en la calle principal. Con relación a los alojamientos, es muy simple, se busca por Booking que tiene muy buenas ofertas, claro está que hay que hacer las reservas con anticipación. Hay presupuestos y presupuesto todo depende como uno se organize y cuánto dinero quería gastar. SUERTE EN VIAJE!!

text_clean: We love the Nazca Lines, a series of ancient geoglyphs located in the Nazca Desert of southern Peru. Created by the Nazca people between 500 BCE and 500 CE, these massive designs cover an area of about 450 square kilometers (170 square miles). Despite many theories, the exact purpose of the Nazca Lines remains a mystery, and researchers continue to explore and debate their significance in the context of Nazca culture and ancient civilizations.

text_clean: "If you're looking for a unique and unforgettable experience in Peru, I highly recommend visiting the Nasca Plateau. The expansive desert landscape is truly awe-inspiring, and the ancient lines etched into the ground will leave you in wonder. It's a testament to the ingenuity and skill of the Nasca people who created them so long ago. The best way to fully appreciate the magnitude and intricacy of the lines is from above, with a scenic flight. The pilots are knowledgeable and provide fascinating insights into the history, culture, and mystery surrounding the lines. Overall, the Nasca Plateau is a must-visit destination for anyone interested in ancient civilizations and mysteries of the world."

text_clean: Machu Picchu Mountain used to be free to hike (with your day-pass to the park), but now comes at an additional charge to your ticket. I'm from Alaska, and no stranger to hiking mountains, 2-3 thousand feet of elevation in an afternoon is no problem for me, so I didn't expect issues on this trail. But hiking at elevation is a different ball-game, it was a completely geuling trek. And due to the new ticketing system, you have to be out of the park by noon (only the morning ticket holders can buy the mountain trail pass) so there's really a rush to get up and down in time to see some of the rest of the park before you have to leave. The trail is poorly managed and cared for with trash everywhere, and even a few spots that have become informal lavatories, and have not been cleaned up in some time. And that isn't even to mention the insanity that is getting to the park in the first place. If you read on the official website not to bring food, plastic water bottles, or trekking polls, forget all that; nobody on the park staff cares about any of the rules, and following them made the hike much more difficult than it would otherwise have had to be, especially with a bad knee that hurts going downhill, I would have loved to have my trekking polls with me, well, plenty of people ignored the rules and were let right in with all their snacks, extra water, and polls. It's not all bad, there are several bluffs and overlooks on the way up that offer absolutely incredible views of the Machu Picchu site, and surrounding valley, some were even better than the view from the peak. And you're hiking on original Inca laid stone, which is an experience in itself. There are a few sections with sheer drops from with the trail maybe 2 feet wide amd there are no guard or hand rails, that's a little hair-raising, but most of the trail is 5 or 6 feet wide. At the top is a small roofless wooden structure with a few benches for resting (the only ones on the entire hike), and a sign with the peak's elevation. That's it. No signage about what the Inca used the top of the mountain for (it's their trail after all), or a map showing the location of other sites visible from the peak and how they related to each other, or anything at all informative, and although you're now required to enter the park with a guide, they don't go up the mountain with you, so no help there. All in all, I don't reccomend the mountain trail unless you're staying at the Belmond and already planning on more than one day in the park. The logistics of getting their are too crazy, and a half-day in the park too expensive to spend it on one hiking trail, the rest of the park is far more interesting anyway.
label: negativo

text_clean: I’m giving a 1-star review for the absolutely terrible new ticketing system. Forcing visitors to see only one tiny section of Machu Picchu is a horrendous idea, and if other world wonders followed this model, world tourism would collapse. My experience was a complete disaster. I originally had a ticket for Circuit No. 2 and wanted to go to the top, but my official tour guide insisted the top was part of Circuit No. 1. So I purchased an additional ticket for Circuit No. 1 just to make sure I could see everything. Then, when I tried to go up, the staff told me that what I wanted to see was not Ticket No. 1 but it was actually Circuit No. 2, and because my original Circuit No. 2 ticket had been valid in the morning, it was now “expired”. So despite buying different tickets, they still refused to let me see the top of circuit 2 from Machu Picchu. Their only response was to tell me to complain to my tour guide, which is absolutely useless and does nothing to fix the fact that I ended up out of one important part I came to see. What makes this even worse is their complete lack of empathy. They don’t understand, or probably don’t care, that people wait months or even years to visit Machu Picchu, and for many it’s a once-in-a-lifetime experience. If someone has a VALID ticket, even if it expired, DURING LOW SEASONS, a few hours earlier due to confusing instructions, the staff should show some humanity and let them in. There is absolutely no reason to be so strict. It was even late afternoon and there wasn't any crowd at all. Instead, the rangers and gate agents were rude, unhelpful, inflexible, and completely indifferent. They just recite the same memorized line "your ticket expired" and refuse to help in any real way. Honestly, AI robots would do a far better job than the staff currently working there. I had heard many complaints about Machu Picchu, but now I've experienced this myself. Hope this review helps someone

There is absolutely no reason to be so strict. It was even late afternoon and there wasn't any crowd at all. Instead, the rangers and gate agents were rude, unhelpful, inflexible, and completely indifferent. They just recite the same memorized line "your ticket expired" and refuse to help in any real way. Honestly, AI robots would do a far better job than the staff currently working there. I had heard many complaints about Machu Picchu, but now I've experienced this myself. Hope this review helps someone

text_clean: ¡Una visita totalmente recomendable!Fuimos un grupo de 4 personas y nos resultó mucho más cómodo y económico desplazarnos desde Trujillo en Uber. El proceso es sencillo: compras la entrada en la boletería, necesitas identificarte (DNI o Pasaporte) y luego caminas unos 200 metros hacia el ingreso. Esa caminata corta es a pleno sol, por lo que es indispensable llevar agua y sombrero, aunque afortunadamente en la entrada misma sí hay sombra para esperar. Esperamos unos 20 minutos a que se formara un grupo para entrar con un guía ( el pago es mediante aportación voluntaria). Un gran detalle es que los mayores de edad cuentan con ticket reducido. Itinerario con rampas, escalones i suelo de arena, no adaptado a sillas de rueddas. La visita dura aproximadamente 1 hora y el recorrido por este templo Moche es súper interesante. Cabe mencionar que la Huaca del Sol no es accesible, pero la Huaca de la Luna compensa todo. ¡Vale muchísimo la pena!

destination: Huacas del Sol y de la Luna
text_clean: Cuál es la mejor temporada para visitar la Huaca del Sol y la Luna? Se puede visitar durante todo el año. Pero, se tiene que tener en cuenta que durante los meses de enero a marzo el clima en Trujillo es bastante caluroso y durante los meses de abril a diciembre es más fresco.Está ubicada al norte del Perú en el departamento de la libertad, a unos 5 km al sur de Trujillo. Se puede visitar este complejo arqueológico de lunes a domingo de 9:00 a.m. hasta las 4:00 p.m. Los tickets de entrada tienen un precio de S/. 10.00 para adultos. Qué llevar: Calzado cómodo, gorro para el sol, anteojos de sol, bloqueador solar, snack, agua y ropa cómoda y un poco de plata..😋 Es obligatorio tener un documento de vacunación con esquema completo.. En mí caso que vivo en tumbes puedo comprar paquetes online por el Tripavisor ó de lo contrario tomar un bus. Tumbes a Trujillo en Bus Oltursa es la principal empresa de transporte que cuenta con esta ruta en su itinerario, ofreciendo pasajes a precios económicos de 70 y 90 soles. Los buses salen desde el terminal., que queda en la calle principal. Con relación a los alojamientos, es muy simple, se busca por Booking que tiene muy buenas ofertas, claro está que hay que hacer las reservas con anticipación. Hay presupuestos y presupuesto todo depende como uno se organize y cuánto dinero quería gastar. SUERTE EN VIAJE!!

text_clean: Machu Picchu Mountain used to be free to hike (with your day-pass to the park), but now comes at an additional charge to your ticket. I'm from Alaska, and no stranger to hiking mountains, 2-3 thousand feet of elevation in an afternoon is no problem for me, so I didn't expect issues on this trail. But hiking at elevation is a different ball-game, it was a completely geuling trek. And due to the new ticketing system, you have to be out of the park by noon (only the morning ticket holders can buy the mountain trail pass) so there's really a rush to get up and down in time to see some of the rest of the park before you have to leave. The trail is poorly managed and cared for with trash everywhere, and even a few spots that have become informal lavatories, and have not been cleaned up in some time. And that isn't even to mention the insanity that is getting to the park in the first place. If you read on the official website not to bring food, plastic water bottles, or trekking polls, forget all that; nobody on the park staff cares about any of the rules, and following them made the hike much more difficult than it would otherwise have had to be, especially with a bad knee that hurts going downhill, I would have loved to have my trekking polls with me, well, plenty of people ignored the rules and were let right in with all their snacks, extra water, and polls. It's not all bad, there are several bluffs and overlooks on the way up that offer absolutely incredible views of the Machu Picchu site, and surrounding valley, some were even better than the view from the peak. And you're hiking on original Inca laid stone, which is an experience in itself. There are a few sections with sheer drops from with the trail maybe 2 feet wide amd there are no guard or hand rails, that's a little hair-raising, but most of the trail is 5 or 6 feet wide. At the top is a small roofless wooden structure with a few benches for resting (the only ones on the entire hike), and a sign with the peak's elevation. That's it. No signage about what the Inca used the top of the mountain for (it's their trail after all), or a map showing the location of other sites visible from the peak and how they related to each other, or anything at all informative, and although you're now required to enter the park with a guide, they don't go up the mountain with you, so no help there. All in all, I don't reccomend the mountain trail unless you're staying at the Belmond and already planning on more than one day in the park. The logistics of getting their are too crazy, and a half-day in the park too expensive to spend it on one hiking trail, the rest of the park is far more interesting anyway.
label: negativo

text_clean: I’m giving a 1-star review for the absolutely terrible new ticketing system. Forcing visitors to see only one tiny section of Machu Picchu is a horrendous idea, and if other world wonders followed this model, world tourism would collapse. My experience was a complete disaster. I originally had a ticket for Circuit No. 2 and wanted to go to the top, but my official tour guide insisted the top was part of Circuit No. 1. So I purchased an additional ticket for Circuit No. 1 just to make sure I could see everything. Then, when I tried to go up, the staff told me that what I wanted to see was not Ticket No. 1 but it was actually Circuit No. 2, and because my original Circuit No. 2 ticket had been valid in the morning, it was now “expired”. So despite buying different tickets, they still refused to let me see the top of circuit 2 from Machu Picchu. Their only response was to tell me to complain to my tour guide, which is absolutely useless and does nothing to fix the fact that I ended up out of one important part I came to see. What makes this even worse is their complete lack of empathy. They don’t understand, or probably don’t care, that people wait months or even years to visit Machu Picchu, and for many it’s a once-in-a-lifetime experience. If someone has a VALID ticket, even if it expired, DURING LOW SEASONS, a few hours earlier due to confusing instructions, the staff should show some humanity and let them in. There is absolutely no reason to be so strict. It was even late afternoon and there wasn't any crowd at all. Instead, the rangers and gate agents were rude, unhelpful, inflexible, and completely indifferent. They just recite the same memorized line "your ticket expired" and refuse to help in any real way. Honestly, AI robots would do a far better job than the staff currently working there. I had heard many complaints about Machu Picchu, but now I've experienced this myself. Hope this review helps someone

text_clean: Machu Picchu Mountain used to be free to hike (with your day-pass to the park), but now comes at an additional charge to your ticket. I'm from Alaska, and no stranger to hiking mountains, 2-3 thousand feet of elevation in an afternoon is no problem for me, so I didn't expect issues on this trail. But hiking at elevation is a different ball-game, it was a completely geuling trek. And due to the new ticketing system, you have to be out of the park by noon (only the morning ticket holders can buy the mountain trail pass) so there's really a rush to get up and down in time to see some of the rest of the park before you have to leave. The trail is poorly managed and cared for with trash everywhere, and even a few spots that have become informal lavatories, and have not been cleaned up in some time. And that isn't even to mention the insanity that is getting to the park in the first place. If you read on the official website not to bring food, plastic water bottles, or trekking polls, forget all that; nobody on the park staff cares about any of the rules, and following them made the hike much more difficult than it would otherwise have had to be, especially with a bad knee that hurts going downhill, I would have loved to have my trekking polls with me, well, plenty of people ignored the rules and were let right in with all their snacks, extra water, and polls. It's not all bad, there are several bluffs and overlooks on the way up that offer absolutely incredible views of the Machu Picchu site, and surrounding valley, some were even better than the view from the peak. And you're hiking on original Inca laid stone, which is an experience in itself. There are a few sections with sheer drops from with the trail maybe 2 feet wide amd there are no guard or hand rails, that's a little hair-raising, but most of the trail is 5 or 6 feet wide. At the top is a small roofless wooden structure with a few benches for resting (the only ones on the entire hike), and a sign with the peak's elevation. That's it. No signage about what the Inca used the top of the mountain for (it's their trail after all), or a map showing the location of other sites visible from the peak and how they related to each other, or anything at all informative, and although you're now required to enter the park with a guide, they don't go up the mountain with you, so no help there. All in all, I don't reccomend the mountain trail unless you're staying at the Belmond and already planning on more than one day in the park. The logistics of getting their are too crazy, and a half-day in the park too expensive to spend it on one hiking trail, the rest of the park is far more interesting anyway.
label: neutro

I would vaguely guess they will need to build a roof over the entire structure at some point to keep stormwater from destabilizing the wall any further. Unless the want to tear sections of the wall down, put a drainage system behind the wall, and rebuild it with a modern support / drainage system behind the walls. Anyways this section of the country is fantastic, and you should totally visit it. But don’t pretend you are going to be able to walk around the top of Kuelap anytime this decade.

Itinerario con rampas, escalones i suelo de arena, no adaptado a sillas de rueddas. La visita dura aproximadamente 1 hora y el recorrido por este templo Moche es súper interesante. Cabe mencionar que la Huaca del Sol no es accesible, pero la Huaca de la Luna compensa todo. ¡Vale muchísimo la pena!

destination: Huacas del Sol y de la Luna
text_clean: Cuál es la mejor temporada para visitar la Huaca del Sol y la Luna? Se puede visitar durante todo el año. Pero, se tiene que tener en cuenta que durante los meses de enero a marzo el clima en Trujillo es bastante caluroso y durante los meses de abril a diciembre es más fresco.Está ubicada al norte del Perú en el departamento de la libertad, a unos 5 km al sur de Trujillo. Se puede visitar este complejo arqueológico de lunes a domingo de 9:00 a.m. hasta las 4:00 p.m. Los tickets de entrada tienen un precio de S/. 10.00 para adultos. Qué llevar: Calzado cómodo, gorro para el sol, anteojos de sol, bloqueador solar, snack, agua y ropa cómoda y un poco de plata..😋 Es obligatorio tener un documento de vacunación con esquema completo.. En mí caso que vivo en tumbes puedo comprar paquetes online por el Tripavisor ó de lo contrario tomar un bus. Tumbes a Trujillo en Bus Oltursa es la principal empresa de transporte que cuenta con esta ruta en su itinerario, ofreciendo pasajes a precios económicos de 70 y 90 soles. Los buses salen desde el terminal., que queda en la calle principal. Con relación a los alojamientos, es muy simple, se busca por Booking que tiene muy buenas ofertas, claro está que hay que hacer las reservas con anticipación. Hay presupuestos y presupuesto todo depende como uno se organize y cuánto dinero quería gastar. SUERTE EN VIAJE!!
label: positivo

text_clean: Machu Picchu Mountain used to be free to hike (with your day-pass to the park), but now comes at an additional charge to your ticket. I'm from Alaska, and no stranger to hiking mountains, 2-3 thousand feet of elevation in an afternoon is no problem for me, so I didn't expect issues on this trail. But hiking at elevation is a different ball-game, it was a completely geuling trek. And due to the new ticketing system, you have to be out of the park by noon (only the morning ticket holders can buy the mountain trail pass) so there's really a rush to get up and down in time to see some of the rest of the park before you have to leave. The trail is poorly managed and cared for with trash everywhere, and even a few spots that have become informal lavatories, and have not been cleaned up in some time. And that isn't even to mention the insanity that is getting to the park in the first place. If you read on the official website not to bring food, plastic water bottles, or trekking polls, forget all that; nobody on the park staff cares about any of the rules, and following them made the hike much more difficult than it would otherwise have had to be, especially with a bad knee that hurts going downhill, I would have loved to have my trekking polls with me, well, plenty of people ignored the rules and were let right in with all their snacks, extra water, and polls. It's not all bad, there are several bluffs and overlooks on the way up that offer absolutely incredible views of the Machu Picchu site, and surrounding valley, some were even better than the view from the peak. And you're hiking on original Inca laid stone, which is an experience in itself. There are a few sections with sheer drops from with the trail maybe 2 feet wide amd there are no guard or hand rails, that's a little hair-raising, but most of the trail is 5 or 6 feet wide. At the top is a small roofless wooden structure with a few benches for resting (the only ones on the entire hike), and a sign with the peak's elevation. That's it. No signage about what the Inca used the top of the mountain for (it's their trail after all), or a map showing the location of other sites visible from the peak and how they related to each other, or anything at all informative, and although you're now required to enter the park with a guide, they don't go up the mountain with you, so no help there. All in all, I don't reccomend the mountain trail unless you're staying at the Belmond and already planning on more than one day in the park. The logistics of getting their are too crazy, and a half-day in the park too expensive to spend it on one hiking trail, the rest of the park is far more interesting anyway.

text_clean: Machu Picchu Mountain used to be free to hike (with your day-pass to the park), but now comes at an additional charge to your ticket. I'm from Alaska, and no stranger to hiking mountains, 2-3 thousand feet of elevation in an afternoon is no problem for me, so I didn't expect issues on this trail. But hiking at elevation is a different ball-game, it was a completely geuling trek. And due to the new ticketing system, you have to be out of the park by noon (only the morning ticket holders can buy the mountain trail pass) so there's really a rush to get up and down in time to see some of the rest of the park before you have to leave. The trail is poorly managed and cared for with trash everywhere, and even a few spots that have become informal lavatories, and have not been cleaned up in some time. And that isn't even to mention the insanity that is getting to the park in the first place. If you read on the official website not to bring food, plastic water bottles, or trekking polls, forget all that; nobody on the park staff cares about any of the rules, and following them made the hike much more difficult than it would otherwise have had to be, especially with a bad knee that hurts going downhill, I would have loved to have my trekking polls with me, well, plenty of people ignored the rules and were let right in with all their snacks, extra water, and polls. It's not all bad, there are several bluffs and overlooks on the way up that offer absolutely incredible views of the Machu Picchu site, and surrounding valley, some were even better than the view from the peak. And you're hiking on original Inca laid stone, which is an experience in itself. There are a few sections with sheer drops from with the trail maybe 2 feet wide amd there are no guard or hand rails, that's a little hair-raising, but most of the trail is 5 or 6 feet wide. At the top is a small roofless wooden structure with a few benches for resting (the only ones on the entire hike), and a sign with the peak's elevation. That's it. No signage about what the Inca used the top of the mountain for (it's their trail after all), or a map showing the location of other sites visible from the peak and how they related to each other, or anything at all informative, and although you're now required to enter the park with a guide, they don't go up the mountain with you, so no help there. All in all, I don't reccomend the mountain trail unless you're staying at the Belmond and already planning on more than one day in the park. The logistics of getting their are too crazy, and a half-day in the park too expensive to spend it on one hiking trail, the rest of the park is far more interesting anyway.

Esa caminata corta es a pleno sol, por lo que es indispensable llevar agua y sombrero, aunque afortunadamente en la entrada misma sí hay sombra para esperar. Esperamos unos 20 minutos a que se formara un grupo para entrar con un guía ( el pago es mediante aportación voluntaria). Un gran detalle es que los mayores de edad cuentan con ticket reducido. Itinerario con rampas, escalones i suelo de arena, no adaptado a sillas de rueddas. La visita dura aproximadamente 1 hora y el recorrido por este templo Moche es súper interesante. Cabe mencionar que la Huaca del Sol no es accesible, pero la Huaca de la Luna compensa todo. ¡Vale muchísimo la pena!

text_clean: Fascinating archaeological site just outside Lima! The museum is modern, clean and very well curated, offering a clear explanation of the history of Pachacamac and the different cultures that lived here — from the Lima and Wari to the Inca civilization. Highly recommended to go with a guide to understand the cultural and spiritual importance of the place.

text_clean: "If you're looking for a unique and unforgettable experience in Peru, I highly recommend visiting the Nasca Plateau. It's a testament to the ingenuity and skill of the Nasca people who created them so long ago. The best way to fully appreciate the magnitude and intricacy of the lines is from above, with a scenic flight. The pilots are knowledgeable and provide fascinating insights into the history, culture, and mystery surrounding the lines. Overall, the Nasca Plateau is a must-visit destination for anyone interested in ancient civilizations and mysteries of the world."

text_clean: Machu Picchu Mountain used to be free to hike (with your day-pass to the park), but now comes at an additional charge to your ticket. I'm from Alaska, and no stranger to hiking mountains, 2-3 thousand feet of elevation in an afternoon is no problem for me, so I didn't expect issues on this trail. But hiking at elevation is a different ball-game, it was a completely geuling trek. And due to the new ticketing system, you have to be out of the park by noon (only the morning ticket holders can buy the mountain trail pass) so there's really a rush to get up and down in time to see some of the rest of the park before you have to leave. The trail is poorly managed and cared for with trash everywhere, and even a few spots that have become informal lavatories, and have not been cleaned up in some time. And that isn't even to mention the insanity that is getting to the park in the first place. If you read on the official website not to bring food, plastic water bottles, or trekking polls, forget all that; nobody on the park staff cares about any of the rules, and following them made the hike much more difficult than it would otherwise have had to be, especially with a bad knee that hurts going downhill, I would have loved to have my trekking polls with me, well, plenty of people ignored the rules and were let right in with all their snacks, extra water, and polls. It's not all bad, there are several bluffs and overlooks on the way up that offer absolutely incredible views of the Machu Picchu site, and surrounding valley, some were even better than the view from the peak. And you're hiking on original Inca laid stone, which is an experience in itself. There are a few sections with sheer drops from with the trail maybe 2 feet wide amd there are no guard or hand rails, that's a little hair-raising, but most of the trail is 5 or 6 feet wide. At the top is a small roofless wooden structure with a few benches for resting (the only ones on the entire hike), and a sign with the peak's elevation. That's it. No signage about what the Inca used the top of the mountain for (it's their trail after all), or a map showing the location of other sites visible from the peak and how they related to each other, or anything at all informative, and although you're now required to enter the park with a guide, they don't go up the mountain with you, so no help there. All in all, I don't reccomend the mountain trail unless you're staying at the Belmond and already planning on more than one day in the park. The logistics of getting their are too crazy, and a half-day in the park too expensive to spend it on one hiking trail, the rest of the park is far more interesting anyway.
label: neutro

text_clean: Machu Picchu Mountain used to be free to hike (with your day-pass to the park), but now comes at an additional charge to your ticket. I'm from Alaska, and no stranger to hiking mountains, 2-3 thousand feet of elevation in an afternoon is no problem for me, so I didn't expect issues on this trail. But hiking at elevation is a different ball-game, it was a completely geuling trek. And due to the new ticketing system, you have to be out of the park by noon (only the morning ticket holders can buy the mountain trail pass) so there's really a rush to get up and down in time to see some of the rest of the park before you have to leave. The trail is poorly managed and cared for with trash everywhere, and even a few spots that have become informal lavatories, and have not been cleaned up in some time. And that isn't even to mention the insanity that is getting to the park in the first place. If you read on the official website not to bring food, plastic water bottles, or trekking polls, forget all that; nobody on the park staff cares about any of the rules, and following them made the hike much more difficult than it would otherwise have had to be, especially with a bad knee that hurts going downhill, I would have loved to have my trekking polls with me, well, plenty of people ignored the rules and were let right in with all their snacks, extra water, and polls. It's not all bad, there are several bluffs and overlooks on the way up that offer absolutely incredible views of the Machu Picchu site, and surrounding valley, some were even better than the view from the peak. And you're hiking on original Inca laid stone, which is an experience in itself. There are a few sections with sheer drops from with the trail maybe 2 feet wide amd there are no guard or hand rails, that's a little hair-raising, but most of the trail is 5 or 6 feet wide. At the top is a small roofless wooden structure with a few benches for resting (the only ones on the entire hike), and a sign with the peak's elevation. That's it. No signage about what the Inca used the top of the mountain for (it's their trail after all), or a map showing the location of other sites visible from the peak and how they related to each other, or anything at all informative, and although you're now required to enter the park with a guide, they don't go up the mountain with you, so no help there. All in all, I don't reccomend the mountain trail unless you're staying at the Belmond and already planning on more than one day in the park. The logistics of getting their are too crazy, and a half-day in the park too expensive to spend it on one hiking trail, the rest of the park is far more interesting anyway.

El camino de la ida pasando por los pueblitos es un poco peligroso pero lindo y la vuelta súper difícil. Hay que prepararse antes de venir acá.

text_clean: I visited the archaeological site today, and although the tour was interesting, I was shocked by the living standards of the animals on the site, present to illustrate the resources of the ancient people. There were ducks with no access to fresh water, which is part of their essential living environment - instead they had a stagnant, filthy puddle and were surrounded by their own waste. The guinea pigs were also living in tiny pens, covered in their own waste. I would ask the site management to please rethink the provisions they are giving to these animals - perhaps even charging a slightly higher ticket price to allow for a better environment. The current living standards are really not appropriate, and lets down an otherwise good value tour.

text_clean: If you already have a general ticket to visit sites that you got elsewhere, bring it! There was a rather long line to buy a ticket at this site. We went around 9am. Glad we did as past noon/1pm it got a bit crowded from more tours arriving. We did the main stops first at higher levels with lower crowds and then did the base level stuff at the end when more crowds arrived as those were less busy. Theres a free bathroom here, but bring toilet paper. If you’re a woman, go to the bathroom early as you’ll not have a line to the bathroom. If you go late, then the line was quite longer.

text_clean: This is a summary of an August visit to Apu Machu Picchu (towards the end of peak season). If you’ve spent at least two days in Cusco, a night in Aguas Calientes, and stayed well hydrated, altitude should not be a problem. I recommend arriving in Aguas Calientes the afternoon before and overnighting—it will make life easier. We did two routes in a single day: the 7 am Huayna Picchu (Circuit 3A) and the 3 pm Classic Route (Circuit 2A). For me, these were the perfect combination to get a comprehensive experience of Machu Picchu, both as a visitor and a photographer. (I would not suggest squeezing in three routes in one day.) Route 2A has the classic postcard views of Machu Picchu with a walk through the main complex. If you can only do one route, do 2A. Route 3A gives you a slightly different perspective from a lower elevation, with the bonus of hiking Huayna Picchu for stunning birdseye views. Both are doable in one day if you plan carefully. For the 7 am 3A Huayna Picchu entry, we lined up for the bus at 6 am in Aguas Calientes and got through the main gate around 7:30. The bus ride itself is 25–30 minutes, but there is also a line at the entrance to factor in. Morning entry is wonderful for photography, with some sun and cloud play. If you are hiking Huayna Picchu, I recommend taking photos on your way up because the lighting on the return is harsher. From the main entrance it took us about 40–45 minutes to reach the Huayna Picchu checkpoint, navigating crowds, guides, and taking photos. The hike itself is challenging but not as dangerous as people make it out to be. It is essentially a continuous staircase with steep grades. The final notorious section known as “Staircase of Death" is not "deadly". Just take it slow and do what you've been doing. Dry conditions are fine but wet weather would make it riskier. At a moderate pace, expect 50-60 mins to reach the top. With frequent stops, around 75 mins. ~45 mins descent. After a quick lunch in Aguas Calientes, we got in line for the bus at 1:30 pm and entered exactly at 3 for the 3 pm 2A classic route. This circuit is not physically demanding beyond walking, and at a relaxed pace it takes around 1.5 to 2 hours. The famous photo terraces are worth waiting for, but there are also excellent viewpoints further down the route with fewer people. The later you stay, the better the lighting and the cleaner your photos. Even with crowds, the photos comes out nice because the site is expansive. We also met a few llamas who were happy to pose, as long as treated respectfully. Do you need a guide? Not necessarily. With some research—or even AI—you can learn as you go and move at your own pace. Guides can add context but they also tend to block paths for their groups, which can be frustrating. The downside of a group is less flexibility to linger in places that speak to you personally. All circuits are one way, so take photos when you see a good spot—you will not be able to loop back. Weather is dependent on cloud cover. If sunny, it'll be warm. If overcast, it'll be chilly. Facilities include restrooms at the entrance (2 soles), luggage storage (5 soles), and a small snack bar, though eating in Aguas Calientes is better. If you book back-to-back slots (like 7 am and 11 am), do not exit the premises in between or you’ll waste time in lines again. Tickets need to be booked on the official government website using your passport details. The process can seem confusing but is fairly straightforward—switching the page back to Spanish often solved website glitches for me. Book at least 2.5-3 months in advance. This is not a place to “wing it.” Each ticket requires passport information (tour operators might not have special last-moment access.) You must carry your physical passport at all times as it is checked against both bus and site tickets. All tickets are digital PDFs with QR codes, which scanned fine from our phones. Overall, we had an incredible visit! We took the 7 pm train back to Cusco and had arranged a late hotel checkout for a small fee for added convenience.
label: positivo

text_clean: This is my third time here and let me tell you there’s been tons of changes. After you get to Aguas Calientes, everyone whether you hired a guide or not must now go to the Ministerio de Culturas and stand in line just to get a number to return later that night or the next day. My understanding from talking to other tour guides is that tour guides used to be able to buy your tickets and they would buy at the Peruvian National price and resell at the international tourist price. To cut down on this corruption now everyone must go and present their passports or their proof of residency (DNI). You are asked how many people in your party and you are given individual numbers for each person. You cannot show up early as they go by the number you were given. Their computer systems are not very stable so expect delays. We were in line a little over 2 hours in the morning and over 3 hours in the evening so almost 6 hours. When your number is called, you go upstairs and have to wait in and move yourself along the winding chairs that lead you back down the stairs to the cashier windows to be asked what circuit you will be getting. There are limited number of tickets per circuit and even though there are “maps” there is nothing showing you what you will see in each circuit and what your pictures will look like. There are screens that can be looked to determine which circuits are still available as you approach the window to place your order. Fee per permit/ticket is $50/person for international traveler regardless of the circuit chosen. Circuit 2A/B is quite popular so for me, I had to go with circuit 3A and that’s the picture I am sharing on this review. Previously on my other 2 visits, I was able to do the whole trip as a day trip and see Machu Picchu and return to Cusco the same day. Now you must stay overnight and some people up to two nights so plan carefully. That said, this area is a lower altitude than Cusco so it is easier to breathe and the town is much more quaint and more enjoyable. That said do not buy your return tickets to Cusco until you have your tour time because you are only allowed to make changes up to 24 hours before and if you try to change at 23 hours and 59 minutes, you will have to buy new tickets. Another piece of advice, when booking bus/train tickets go with Peru Rail instead of Inca Rail as the train cars are much nicer and have wider aisles and better “shows”. With these changes, it almost makes it not worth it to even visit. To me, Machu Picchu is completely overrated and overpriced and inconvenient to get to. Although I’m glad I’ve come 3 times (first time 2021 during covid and raining so pictures were not adequate, second time 2023 with niece and third time 2025 with my wife because she wanted to visit), I’ve thought this every time I’ve come.

text_clean: De reserva solo tiene el nombre y lo que te cobran en la entrada. Apenas hay carteles informativos sobre las especies y su estado de conservación, época de cría o paso por la reserva. Hay pescadores pescando con red, varias personas retirando las algas de la playa ( no sé si es por turismo o pesca) que son ecosistemas super importantes para mucha fauna que vive en ellas. Las carreteras dan lástima, los carros y bugys se meten en la playa y por las dunas ( donde viven y anidan muchas especies) y para colmo el centro de interpretación está cerrado por "mantenimiento" . El baño tampoco está incluido en el precio de la entrada. Una vergüenza de reserva, solo es un saca dinero para los turistas. Los paisajes y las playas son muy bonitas y hay muchas aves en la zona

text_clean: So disappointed to have made a 40 minute journey across town to see Huaca Pucilana after reading about the site in an official leaflet, only to be told that you need a prior appointment that wasn't advertised on the leaflet I had. Upon showing the leaflet to the attendant, I'd have thought they would have showed a little more consideration given the leaflet was one of their own. Unfortunately there are times when common sense is outweighed by someone wearing a uniform, and they'd much prefer to sit around twiddling their thumbs. A walk around the site enabled us to get an idea of the sheer scale of the place, and to witness a guide showing only 2 people around. Really busy. To see their llamas caged in to such a small area when there's so much space for them to roam is a complete mystery to me. Disgraceful mistreatment of animals.

text_clean: It might sound insane to rate this site as 2 star consider is a world heritage site but this was one of the most deflating experiences of my trip. We got the circuito 1 ticket which basically keeps you out of the site and you get is a view. AND NOTICE ONCE UP THERE YOU CANNOT BUY A SECOND TICKET . When we realized this we tried to get another ticket but we were told we needed to go down to the town and buy another one and then go back up again. Which means a couple of hours by bus and your train tickets might be lost as they also have set times. To be honest they should not sell this ticket! thank God they sun came out and we had a site other wise we would have spend $300 to see a cloud! I really dislike how artificial and turistic this attraction is and how circuito 1 feels more like a scam

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: El museo genial, toda la historia y la cultura formidable. Punto en contra y que hizo la experiencia negativa fue el tema de los guias. Nos hicieron pasar y dentro nos separaron poco cortésmente en grupos con guía y sin guía. A las personas que no teníamos guía, nos pedían que "nos organicemos y contratemos uno y que vale 30 soles". Y así nos dejaron, sin más en medio de todo. El problema no fue el monto, el problema es la forma. Hasta pudimos haber pagado un poco más en la boletería, pero no dejarnos en el medio de una sala a oscuras y "a que nos organicemos".
label: neutro
